metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the path of the new held-out unit test (e.g. `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py`) to the `Files:` list so the executor has a canonical location and CI coverage is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The held-out unit test for AC3 (section-bounded placement assertions for sign_review, reconcile, fsck, run_workflow) is not listed in the `Files:` inventory and has no specified path, forcing the executor to invent one; the parity test (`test_ci_workflow_parity.py`) won't catch a misplaced or missing test file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; `Files:` block at end of plan
findings.suggested_fix: Append `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py` (new) to the `Files:` line and add one sentence: 'The test calls `gen_mcp_reference.py` (or equivalent fixture) to ensure `docs/mcp-reference.md` is current before slicing it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named test file (e.g. `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py`) to the Files list that will contain the section-bounded held-out unit test described in AC3.
findings.finding: The held-out unit test mandated by AC3 (section-bounded assertions for sign_review placement, reconcile/fsck/run_workflow annotations) is described in detail in the AC but is absent from the plan's Files list. Without a named file path, this deliverable is effectively untracked in the edit-set and easy to omit during implementation or code review.
findings.location: AC line 3 / Files section at bottom of plan
findings.suggested_fix: Append `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py` (new) to the Files list, and confirm AC3's section-bounded assertions are implemented there.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the unit test file path (e.g., `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py`) to the Files section so the AC 3 deliverable is fully specified and traceable.
findings.finding: The held-out unit test described in AC 3 ('a held-out unit test asserts, using section-bounded slicing of docs/mcp-reference.md') is a deliverable with no file path and is absent from the Files section at the bottom of the plan.
findings.location: AC 3 ('a held-out unit test asserts') vs. Files section ('scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py (new), docs/mcp-reference.md (new/generated), .github/workflows/test.yml, .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml')
findings.suggested_fix: Append `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py` (new) to the Files list at the bottom of the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the section-bounded unit test file (e.g. `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py`) to the `Files:` deliverable list, specifying its path and confirming it is distinct from `test_ci_workflow_parity.py`.
findings.finding: The section-bounded unit test mandated by AC-3 (asserting sign_review placement, reconcile/fsck/run_workflow annotations) is described in detail but is NOT listed as a deliverable file in the 'Files:' section. An implementer following the file list will not create this test, leaving the correctness of the special-case classification table unverified.
findings.location: Files list at plan bottom vs. AC bullet 3 ('held-out unit test asserts, using section-bounded slicing')
findings.suggested_fix: Append `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py` (new) to the Files list and add a brief description: 'Contains section-bounded assertions for sign_review, reconcile, fsck, run_workflow placement per AC-3; does not re-invoke the generator — reads the committed docs/mcp-reference.md and checks hardcoded expected strings.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact FastMCP Python API for reading a registered tool's name and description after registration (e.g., inspect `mcp._tool_manager.tools`, `mcp.list_tools()`, or the tool callable's `__doc__`) and pin the specific attribute path in the generator spec or provide a code sketch confirming it works against the installed FastMCP version.
findings.finding: The plan asserts tool descriptions are extracted via `.description` on each registered FastMCP tool object, but the specific FastMCP internal API for post-registration tool introspection (e.g., `mcp._tool_manager.tools`, a sync `list_tools()`, or a `.description` attribute path) is unverified against the installed FastMCP version. If the attribute path is wrong, the generator silently produces empty descriptions for all 47 tools with no error, making the generated doc useless without a noisy failure.
findings.location: Scope section: 'read each tool's name + first-line summary (from `.description`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the generator, after calling `register_*_tools(mcp, ctx)`, access tools via `mcp._tool_manager.tools` (a dict of name→Tool) or the equivalent FastMCP-version-appropriate accessor, and fall back to reading the original function's `__doc__` (which is always accessible since the registrars use plain Python `def` inside closures decorated with `@mcp.tool`). The plan could also specify using `inspect.getdoc(fn)` on the decorated function before decoration, avoiding FastMCP internals entirely — since the tool closures' docstrings are always the source of truth.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the count discrepancy: change 'The three special cases' in the Scope section to 'The four special cases' (or vice-versa, reconcile with Source of Truth which explicitly states FOUR entries).
findings.finding: The Scope section says 'The three special cases are inline ANNOTATIONS' but then lists four items (reconcile, fsck, sign_review, run_workflow), and the Source of Truth section explicitly states 'This special-case table is CLOSED at FOUR entries (reconcile, fsck, sign_review, run_workflow).' An implementer reading the Scope would annotate only three special cases, potentially omitting one.
findings.location: Scope section ('The three special cases are inline ANNOTATIONS') vs. Source of Truth section ('CLOSED at FOUR entries')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, replace 'The three special cases are inline ANNOTATIONS' with 'The four special cases are inline ANNOTATIONS' to match the Source of Truth section's explicit 'CLOSED at FOUR entries' statement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete home for the 'four-name registrar membership' drift assertion — e.g., 'add an assertion block at the top of `gen_mcp_reference.py`'s `main()` that verifies `sign_review` is enumerated from `_mcp_llm`, `reconcile`/`fsck` from `_mcp_reads`, and `run_workflow` from `_mcp_writes`, raising with a clear message if any is missing' — so an executor knows exactly where to put it.
findings.finding: The 'drift check asserts these four names still exist in their expected registrars' is a named processing protocol referenced in the Classification rule section but never assigned to a concrete implementation home — the plan does not say whether this assertion lives inside gen_mcp_reference.py (at generation time), inside its --check path, inside the held-out unit test named in AC#3, or as a standalone CI step. An executor cannot implement it without guessing.
findings.location: Plan section: 'Classification rule' — '...a drift check asserts these four names still exist in their expected registrars, so a new hybrid is caught rather than silently misclassified.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add a bullet: 'At the top of `gen_mcp_reference.py`'s generation logic (before emitting any output), assert that the four closed-table names (`reconcile`, `fsck`, `sign_review`, `run_workflow`) are present in their expected registrar tool lists; raise `SystemExit` with a diagnostic if any is missing or has moved registrars, so the gate fails rather than silently misclassifies.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact FastMCP API the generator uses to read back registered tools and their descriptions (e.g., `mcp._tool_map`, `mcp.tools`, or a sync wrapper around `list_tools()`), and note whether this attribute is stable across the pinned FastMCP version range — or consider an AST-based approach for registrar source files to avoid internal-API coupling entirely.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the generator should 'read each tool's name + first-line summary (from `.description`)' after registering each registrar onto its own FastMCP instance, but does not specify WHICH FastMCP introspection API the generator uses to retrieve those values. FastMCP's internal tool registry (e.g., `_tool_map`, `_tools`, or similar) is a private/version-varying implementation detail; the async protocol method `list_tools()` requires an event loop. The analogous `gen_env_registry.py` avoids this entirely by using AST parsing. Without pinning the specific API surface, the implementer may choose a broken or version-fragile approach (e.g., `await mcp.list_tools()` in a sync context, or accessing a private attribute that moves between FastMCP releases), causing the generator to fail at runtime or silently miss tools.
findings.location: Scope section — 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP … read each tool's name + first-line summary (from `.description`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the generator's self-check to detect potential new hybrids: e.g., assert that any tool with `destructiveHint=True` or `readOnlyHint=False` in the READS registrar is present in the override table, or document a required manual step and add a TODO comment in the generator for future maintainers.
findings.finding: The closed special-case override table (4 entries: reconcile, fsck, sign_review, run_workflow) is hardcoded in the generator with a drift check that only asserts those 4 names still exist in their expected registrars — it does NOT detect when a NEW hybrid tool is added without a corresponding table entry, so a future developer adding a write-side-effect tool to _mcp_reads.py or an LLM-call tool to _mcp_writes.py would silently receive the wrong gate classification in the generated doc.
findings.location: Scope section — classification rule / closed override table; scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py (new)
findings.suggested_fix: In gen_mcp_reference.py, after enumerating READS tools, assert that every tool whose FastMCP annotations carry destructiveHint=True (or readOnlyHint=False) is present in the SPECIAL_CASES dict; raise an error with instructions to update the table if a new one is found. This makes the 'closed' invariant machine-enforceable rather than documentation-only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default for a missing or empty `.description`: e.g., 'use an empty string / emit `(no description)` as the summary cell, never raise'.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a default behavior when a tool's `.description` attribute is `None` or an empty string, leaving the executor to guess whether to raise, skip, or substitute a placeholder.
findings.location: Scope section — 'read each tool's name + first-line summary (from `.description`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Scope bullet: 'If `.description` is `None` or empty, emit `(no description)` as the summary cell.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/README.md` to the Files list and update it (under the Agent or Operator section) with an entry for `docs/mcp-reference.md`, following the same pattern as the existing `env-vars.md` entry.
findings.finding: docs/README.md is an unenumerated consumer that will be left stale. It is a curated, audience-grouped index of every docs/ file, with explicit subsections for Operator/Agent pages. Adding docs/mcp-reference.md without also updating docs/README.md leaves the index incomplete — the pattern established by docs/env-vars.md (which has an entry in docs/README.md) is not followed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to docs/README.md's Agent (or Operator) section: `- **[mcp-reference.md](mcp-reference.md)** — the generated reference of all 47 MCP tools, classified by gate tier (kept in sync by a CI drift gate).`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in AC3 (and in the test's docstring) that the test must invoke the generator (or a subprocess call to `gen_mcp_reference.py`) as a pytest fixture/setup step before slicing the output, so the oracle always exercises live generator output.
findings.finding: The AC3 unit test reads `docs/mcp-reference.md` from disk, but the plan does not specify how the test ensures the file is present and up-to-date before it runs — a missing prerequisite-state ordering gap (taxonomy item 7).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'The test calls `subprocess.run([sys.executable, "scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py"], check=True)` (or a direct function call) in its setup, then reads the freshly written `docs/mcp-reference.md` for section-bounded assertions.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief entry to CLAUDE.md (or the scripts/ README if one exists) describing the gen_mcp_reference.py generator pattern and instructing contributors to update the SPECIAL_CASES override table when adding hybrid-gated tools.
findings.finding: No update to CLAUDE.md, README, or any contributor-facing design doc is planned to explain the generator pattern (gen_mcp_reference.py) and — critically — the process for maintaining the closed special-case override table when adding new tools with hybrid gating, leaving future contributors without guidance on when and how to extend it.
findings.suggested_fix: In CLAUDE.md under a 'Generated artifacts' or 'Scripts' section, add: 'When adding an MCP tool with hybrid gating (gated differently from its registrar default), update the SPECIAL_CASES table in scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py and re-run the generator.' This mirrors the doc pattern that gen_env_registry.py presumably already has.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that gen_mcp_reference.py writes to a temp file and atomically renames it to docs/mcp-reference.md (or equivalent) so a partial write never leaves a corrupt committed file.
findings.finding: The generator writes docs/mcp-reference.md non-atomically; if the process is interrupted mid-write (OOM, SIGKILL, disk full), a partial/corrupt file is committed to the repo and could silently pass a subsequent --check if the truncation happens to preserve the header.
findings.location: Scope section — 'emit docs/mcp-reference.md'; AC drift-gate check
findings.suggested_fix: In the script spec, add: write output to `docs/mcp-reference.md.tmp`, then `os.replace('docs/mcp-reference.md.tmp', 'docs/mcp-reference.md')` which is atomic on POSIX. In `--check` mode, generate to an in-memory buffer and compare, never touching the file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that gen_mcp_reference.py wraps each registrar import/enumeration in a try/except and emits a named diagnostic (e.g., 'ERROR: writes registrar failed to load: <reason>') before re-raising, so CI failures are immediately actionable.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify error handling when a registrar module fails to import or raises during tool registration (e.g., a missing transitive dependency, a NameError introduced by a refactor); the generator would exit with an unguided Python traceback and CI would fail without a clear diagnostic.
findings.location: Scope section — 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify --check mode behavior when docs/mcp-reference.md is absent: either treat it as a drift failure with a clear message ('docs/mcp-reference.md not found — run gen_mcp_reference.py to generate it') and exit non-zero, or auto-generate and then fail on diff.
findings.finding: The --check mode's behavior when docs/mcp-reference.md does not yet exist (e.g., on a fresh branch before the file is first generated) is unspecified; a FileNotFoundError would produce a confusing traceback rather than a clear 'file not found — run gen_mcp_reference.py first' message.
findings.location: Scope section — '--check drift mode'; AC line: 'python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py --check'
findings.suggested_fix: In --check mode: if the target file does not exist, print 'docs/mcp-reference.md is missing; run: python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py' to stderr and exit 1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the generator must assert the enumerated tool count from each registrar matches the expected counts (23 read, 6 llm, 18 write) immediately after registration and before emitting output, raising with a diagnostic on mismatch, so an import/registration failure fails fast rather than silently emitting a partial reference.
findings.finding: No fallback or fail-fast is specified for the case where a registrar raises during the generator's live-enumeration step. Unlike gen_env_registry.py (which is a pure AST scan), gen_mcp_reference.py must import and call register_read_tools / register_llm_tools / register_write_tools on live FastMCP instances; an import failure, missing 'mcp' extra, or ctx wiring error would cause the generator to emit a partial or empty reference that then passes --check on subsequent runs (since the stale committed file also contains fewer tools). The count AC (#4) is post-hoc and does not fail the generator itself.
findings.location: Plan section: Scope — 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0 (writes register)'

findings.finding: The plan's 'closed 3-entry special-case override table' omits run_workflow, which lives in the WRITE registrar but also has a call-time REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM gate (when the workflow has LLM steps and dry_run=False). The plan asserts the table is closed/complete, but the code shows a fourth hybrid case. A generator following the plan's classification table would silently annotate run_workflow as purely REBAR_MCP_READONLY-gated, producing a misleading reference entry.
findings.location: Source of truth section — Classification rule table; also Scope section ('three special cases are inline ANNOTATIONS')
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the override table with a row: `run_workflow` -> `REBAR_MCP_READONLY` (registration) + conditional `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM` at call-time (for LLM-step workflows); annotate in the Write-gated section as 'also requires REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM for workflows with LLM steps (dry_run=False)'.

findings.finding: The plan's annotation for `reconcile` specifies only `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC` as the live-mode gate, but the code gates mutating modes on BOTH `_readonly()` AND `_allow_jira_sync()` (readonly is checked first). A generator following the plan will annotate reconcile as 'live mode gated by REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC' without mentioning that REBAR_MCP_READONLY also blocks mutating modes — leaving users incorrectly believing they can run live reconcile on a readonly server if JIRA_SYNC is set.
findings.location: Source of truth section — Classification rule table, READS row; AC item 3 ('reconcile row text contains REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC')
findings.suggested_fix: Change the override table entry to: `reconcile` -> annotated 'mutating modes gated by both `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC` and `REBAR_MCP_READONLY`; dry-run/check modes always available'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
